behaviors.description: The wait time between retries doubles with each consecutive failure, up to a configured maximum.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: this is primarily backoff math/state progression, testable at one policy/function boundary. REAL-INFRA/MOCK-HIDE: no real OS/network/subprocess is required; fake timers or pure timestamp arithmetic is enough. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none. Adversarial critique: if retry-attempt state is only maintained across the real retry executor, a pure function test could miss integration bugs. Top-2 contrast (runner-up Integration): behavior text focuses on doubling/cap formula itself, not cross-module handoff, so Unit is the minimum. Rejection-gate: System is disqualified because behavior text does not require real HTTP/timer primitives, only correct interval calculation.
behaviors.test_description: Unit-test `computeBackoff(attempt, base, max)` (or equivalent policy object) with consecutive failure counts and assert sequence (e.g., 100,200,400,... capped at max) and cap saturation behavior.

behaviors.description: The retry logic correctly identifies a rate-limit response and computes the appropriate wait time before the next attempt.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: failure commonly appears at module boundaries (HTTP response adapter -> retry classifier -> backoff calculator). REAL-INFRA: real network is not required; crafted in-process responses can exercise the boundary. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) no real OS/network necessity, (b) no AI output dependency, (c) no multi-agentic chain, (d) no LLM stage handoff. Adversarial critique: if rate-limit detection is a single pure predicate, Unit might suffice. Top-2 contrast (runner-up Unit): behavior says 'retry logic identifies response and computes wait', implying composed logic across components, which Unit on one helper can miss. Rejection-gate: System is disqualified by absence of any requirement for real HTTP conditions/timeouts.
behaviors.test_description: Wire retry middleware with a stub transport returning a realistic 429/rate-limit payload+headers; assert classifier marks it retryable and computed delay matches policy/header precedence.

behaviors.description: After the wait, the client actually resends the original request and returns the successful result to the caller transparently.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: this verifies orchestration across timer/backoff, request replay, and response propagation. REAL-INFRA/MOCK-HIDE: can be caught with fake clock + stub transport; real network/process adds little for this behavior text. INTEGRATION-BRAKE checks all negative for System/Agentic/Workflow triggers. Adversarial critique: if retry-resend is implemented in one function, a Unit test with spies could be enough. Top-2 contrast (runner-up Unit): 'resends original request' plus 'returns successful result transparently' requires interaction between retry scheduler and transport boundary, making Integration safer minimum. Rejection-gate: System rejected because behavior does not require real socket semantics, only correct resend/orchestration.
behaviors.test_description: Use fake timers and a transport stub that fails once with rate-limit then succeeds; assert the same request payload/options are resent after advanced time and final resolved value equals successful response body.

behaviors.description: After the configured maximum number of retries, the client returns a typed `QuotaExhaustedError` rather than a generic error.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: correctness depends on retry loop exhaustion state being handed to error-mapping logic. REAL-INFRA: no real HTTP/OS needed; deterministic stub failures suffice. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: no System/Agentic/Workflow trigger. Adversarial critique: if there is a standalone `mapError()` helper, Unit could test the type mapping. Top-2 contrast (runner-up Unit): behavior is conditioned on 'after maximum retries', which requires loop-state integration, not just static mapping. Rejection-gate: higher System level disqualified since no real infra behavior is required by text.
behaviors.test_description: Stub transport to always return rate-limit; configure max retries N; run `call()` and assert rejection is `instanceof QuotaExhaustedError` with expected typed fields, not generic error class.

behaviors.description: The full retry chain — initial call, backoff retries, quota tracking — terminates correctly and does not loop indefinitely before surfacing the error.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: this is end-to-end within library modules (retry executor + backoff + quota state), where infinite-loop bugs emerge from interaction. REAL-INFRA/MOCK-HIDE: fake clock and bounded stub responses can expose non-termination without real network. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: no mandatory System/Agentic/Workflow indicators. Adversarial critique: timer/event-loop edge cases in real runtime could justify System in some implementations. Top-2 contrast (runner-up System): behavior text targets library chain termination logic, not OS/network timing anomalies, so Integration is minimum. Rejection-gate: System disqualified because infinite-loop condition can be reproduced with in-process controlled scheduler.
behaviors.test_description: Run `call()` with fake timers and always-rate-limited stub; assert total attempts equals expected bound and promise settles (rejects) within bounded virtual time, never issuing unbounded retries.

behaviors.description: Requests issued concurrently from multiple async callers in the same process all draw from the same shared quota bucket.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: failure is at shared-state/module interaction boundary under concurrency (multiple callers + quota manager). Unit on one function misses cross-call coordination bugs. REAL-INFRA: no real network/OS primitive required; in-process async concurrency reproduces it. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a)(b)(c)(d) all no. Adversarial critique: if quota bucket is just one atomic object, Unit tests on that object might catch most issues. Top-2 contrast (runner-up Unit): behavior explicitly spans 'multiple async callers', requiring wiring through public call path to prove shared bucket usage. Rejection-gate: System disqualified because requirement is same-process coordination, not external infra behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Create one client instance with shared quota state and launch concurrent `call()` invocations via controlled stub transport; assert aggregate token consumption/429 handling reflects one shared bucket rather than per-caller independent quotas.

behaviors.description: When multiple requests are waiting to be sent, they are dispatched in the order they were originally submitted.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: ordering bugs emerge from queue + scheduler + dispatcher interaction, not isolated helper logic alone. REAL-INFRA/MOCK-HIDE: deterministic in-process queue tests with controlled async timing can reveal reordering; real OS/network not required. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: no System/Agentic/Workflow triggers. Adversarial critique: if dispatch order is a pure queue data-structure property, Unit could be enough. Top-2 contrast (runner-up Unit): behavior references pending requests being 'waiting to be sent', implying orchestration timing and dispatch pipeline, which is integration-level. Rejection-gate: System rejected because no requirement for real socket scheduling or kernel-level ordering appears in behavior text.
behaviors.test_description: Enqueue multiple calls rapidly while send path is blocked, then unblock and record dispatch sequence in transport stub; assert FIFO order matches submission order under concurrent awaiters.
